author: @MrDoyley35
text:
I had an idea once to use cheap components to make a generator from solar thermal energy. (Cheap solar thermal collector, fridge compressors etc)
Low temperature differentials were the main issue for collecting low grade but high availability energy.
How much can that be mitigated at the heat exchanger interface. I was thinking of a 3D printed heat exchangers with a massive surface area and high flow rate to be incorporated in an orc system. Never could work out the maths to explore this potential though!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It's a good idea - i have seen quite a few solar thermal stirling engines - mostly the ones i have seen have been just large LTD engines

author: @Buzzhumma
text:
From old mate chatgpt .....An electron exhibits paramagnetic behavior, meaning it has an unpaired magnetic moment and tends to align with external magnetic fields. This is due to its intrinsic property of spin, which generates a magnetic dipole moment. Electrons with unpaired spins contribute to paramagnetism in materials, while paired electrons (which cancel each other’s magnetic moments) do not.

In addition, an electron can be described as having both spin angular momentum and orbital angular momentum, both of which contribute to its magnetic properties. However, individually, an unpaired electron behaves as a paramagnetic entity.

author: @makerslife
text:
Hey Rob, it's so good to see you back at it again! I have a thought for you to eliminate the dead area of a coil while generating.  The stator is a coil  wound in small loops around the diameter of a larger circle in the shape of like a daisy. The rotor is a planetary gearbox with the alternating field magnets on the planet gears. With the correct gearing and coil arrangement, the magnetic field could stay perpendicular to the wire through the full rotation of the cycle. What do you think?

V/R,
Kyle

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Sounds good to me mate - but axial flux does a good job and is pretty easy to make

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @ChaseFreedomMusician
text:
I think also one thing people lose sight of is the concept many hands make light work. So let's say it is "easy" for you to make a generator that is only 60% efficient but you can make one in a weekend, and you enjoy the process. Well in 2 weekends you made something that is 120% as "effective" as something the original size in much less time because you just doubled up the easy thing. Generators can run in parallel, you can have lots of them and the easier and cheaper they are to build the more of them you can have. 

It sounds really obvious and almost daft when you say it out loud, but it seems like everybody is just on a quest to build "one thing" as in if I just build this "one thing" then all of my needs will be met, but that one thing is usually rediculously difficult or expensive to build. So why not build 10 things for a fraction of the price and effort?

replies.author: @ChaseFreedomMusician
replies.text: This is one of those areas I think 3d printing really shines at it's best. Is the ability to make jigs to make things like doing windings a pretty trivial task.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @DM-kl4em
text:
Sorry, just one more comment:
The other thing about generators is that they actually get harder to spin, the faster you spin them.  This is because it converts mechanical energy into electrical energy, and this mechanical energy comes from the spinning of the generator.  At low speeds, a hand-crank generator may feel like you are stirring a cup of tea.  As you crank faster, it may start to feel like you are stirring a cup of molasses.  It all goes to show that "generators are not generators"; they are converters.

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: They don't get harder to spin the faster you spin them. They get harder to spin when the current increases because of the load. If the load increases they get harder to spin. If there is no load they can spin infinitely fast with hardly any effort. Aka Lenz's law.

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:
​@justtinkering6713 he might be saying that as the speed increases.. the load such as a big battery might try sucking more current.. and will increase the torque required to spin the turbine and whatnot.
Thus, the increased difficulty to spin the generator.

replies.author: @yasirrakhurrafat1142
replies.text:
​@justtinkering6713 if the load is non existent, or requires a limited amount of constant energy input. Then ofcourse, the resistance in turning the generator to faster speeds wouldn't rise exponentially.
But if the load is greedy for energy, of course the resistance/torque requirement may increase exponentially.

replies.author: @DM-kl4em
replies.text:
You are correct.  I misspoke.  As load increases, the generator has to spin faster to maintain adequate voltage.  In the case of a hand crank generator, this requires a higher gear ratio, and consequently, higher mechanical resistance at input.  The human arm can't spin things very fast, so we have to make up for it with gear ratios and brute strength.

On a side note, if you made a generator with a long lever arm that travels in a circle, you could add a harness and have it pulled in a circle by a horse.  I have not confirmed this, but you can allegedly supply up to 99 percent of 746 watts in this manner. 😁

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: @DM-kl4em Also , to gain the most power from your generator the load impedance should match the generator impedance.  That is why you need to know your load before designing your generator.

Ok, I’ll try to briefly describe. 
But first, there are no Pelton wheel motor at present. But…
A large vertical Pipe, say 30cm X 3m, with a Pelton wheel motor at the bottom that feeds into a smaller pipe that ascends vertically to another Pelton wheel motor at the top that returns to the first large pipe. The small ascending pipe has turbines on it, of which, the output of some turbines is directed towards the Pelton wheel motors that drive the mass of water that is contained within the arrangement, while the others are “free” to deliver current to an external load. 
The mass of the water in the large pipe, coupled with the siphoning, should be sufficient to assist the motors easily upwards to give power to the turbines, enough so that the work being done would be continuous for the life of the parts of the machine. 
It would look like a vertical band saw. 
If nothing else, the thought is amusing. 
Thanks for all your dedication!

author: @DM-kl4em
text:
One mind-boggling experiment, that you can try at home, is to take a copper tube, and a magnet just small enough to drop down the tube.

Try dropping the magnet on the table by itself, and THEN try dropping it down the tube.  You will be amazed at how much slower the magnet is when falling through the tube.

This is because the magnet uses some of its energy to excite the electrons in the copper tube.  Since energy cannot be created or destroyed, this energy has to come from the falling of the magnet due to gravity.  Consequently, the magnet falls a lot more slowly when dropped through the copper tube.

replies.author: @Roguescienceguy
replies.text: AKA the Lenz effect

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
